column2: Before installing or replacing an internal computer power supply, what safety step should a technician take first?
column4: Disconnect the system from its power source before working inside it.
column5:
Why

Power supplies handle hazardous electricity, and some devices can store charge after being unplugged. Follow the system documentation before touching components that may retain charge.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic Power Safety Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the basic electrical role of a desktop computer power supply?
column4: It converts AC wall power into the DC voltages used by internal computer components.
column5:
Distinction

The input side is regional wall power. The output side is the low-voltage DC power distributed inside the computer.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic Power Voltage Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop with a manual input-voltage selector is being installed in a region that uses 220-240 VAC. What must be checked before powering it on?
column3: Match the supply input to the wall power before applying power.
column4: Verify that the power supply is set for the correct input-voltage range, 220-240 VAC, or that it supports that range automatically.
column5:
Why

Input voltage is selected on the AC side of the power supply. A wrong setting can prevent operation or damage the unit.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Voltage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the practical advantage of an auto-voltage power supply when a computer may be used in different countries?
column4: It can use different supported input-voltage ranges without the technician manually setting a voltage switch.
column5:
Distinction

Auto-voltage input helps with regional AC differences. It does not remove the need to check connectors, wattage, efficiency, or component requirements.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Voltage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why does Objective 3.6 compare 3.3 V, 5 V, and 12 V power-supply outputs?
column4: Different internal components use different DC output voltages, so the power supply must provide the required rails for the system.
column5:
Why

The useful exam distinction is not a complete rail table. It is recognizing that a PSU must provide the correct DC outputs for the motherboard, storage, cooling, and adapters.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Voltage Source::Messer-v170

column2: Which power-supply output is most associated with higher-power devices such as PCIe adapters, drive motors, and cooling fans?
column5:
Why

The 12 V rail is commonly tied to motors, fans, high-power adapters, and many modern components. The objective still expects awareness of 3.3 V and 5 V outputs as well.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic Power Voltage Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why do many ATX power supplies use a 20+4-pin motherboard power connector?
column4: The extra four-pin section lets the connector support either older 20-pin motherboards or newer 24-pin motherboard power sockets when attached.
column5:
Distinction

The 20+4-pin connector is the main motherboard power connector. It is separate from auxiliary PCIe power connectors used by some high-power add-on cards.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Connector Source::Messer-v170

column2: When is a redundant power supply the appropriate choice?
column4: Use redundant power supplies when the system must keep running after one power supply module fails, such as in server hardware.
column5:
Why

Redundancy is about availability, not extra performance. Each supply must be able to support the load so a failed module can be replaced without stopping the supported system.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.40; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Redundancy Server Source::Messer-v170

column2: What is the main installation advantage of a modular power supply over a fixed-cable power supply?
column4: A modular power supply lets the technician attach only the cables needed for the build, reducing unused cabling and improving cable management and airflow.
column5:
Tradeoff

Modular cabling is cleaner but can cost more. Fixed-cable supplies may leave unused cables in the case or may not provide every cable a build needs.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.41; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Modular Source::Messer-v170

column2: When selecting a replacement power supply for a desktop with a high-performance video adapter, what should the technician verify?
column3: The graphics adapter often drives the power budget.
column4: Verify that the PSU wattage and required power connectors meet the system and video adapter requirements.
column5:
Why

Video adapters are often the largest power draw in a desktop. Component documentation commonly lists a recommended power supply wattage.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.41; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Wattage Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Why is simply choosing the highest-wattage power supply not always the best answer?
column4: A larger PSU does not make the computer faster, can cost more, and may not operate at its most efficient point for the actual load.
column5:
Distinction

Appropriate wattage means enough capacity for the components and reasonable growth, not the largest number available.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.41; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic Power Wattage Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does higher power-supply energy efficiency mean in practical terms?
column4: More of the AC input becomes usable DC output, with less power wasted as heat.
column5:
Why

Efficiency affects heat and operating cost. It does not replace checking wattage, connector support, or input-voltage compatibility.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.41; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 3.6
column10: A+::220-1201::3.6 A+::220-1201::Domain3-Hardware A+::220-1201::PowerSupplies Basic HighYield Power Efficiency Source::Messer-v170
